items.q: What is an iterative design process?
items.correct: A cyclical process where designs are repeatedly tested, evaluated, and refined.
items.misconception: A linear process from brief to final product.
items.random_wrong: A process used only for software.
items.why: Students learn a 'step 1, step 2' sequence and apply it to iteration too. Iteration loops back - you might redesign after testing, then test again, many times.

items.correct: Designing products usable by the widest possible range of people without special adaptation.
items.random_wrong: Adding multiple language options to packaging.
items.why: Students conflate inclusive with specialist design. Inclusive design widens MAINSTREAM products to work for more people - the OXO Good Grips kitchen tools, designed for arthritic hands, work better for everyone.

items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.
The answer examiners want is: Specifications. “Drawings” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Nielsen's heuristics for usability were published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1994. “1984” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters in the ACCESS-FM acronym (counting all)?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
